factual-accuracy.requirement: States HCI timing guidelines approximately 0.1s instantaneous, 1s preserves thought, beyond 10s loses attention
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses latency thresholds like sub-300 ms and mentions 1 second in a few places, but it never states the classic HCI timing guidelines of ~0.1s as instantaneous, ~1s preserving thought, and >10s losing attention. The specific 0.1s and 10s benchmarks are absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States inline suggestions should target 150-300ms first render to preserve flow state
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends maintaining “sub-300 ms latency” for inline suggestions and discusses preserving flow, but it does not explicitly state that inline suggestions should target a 150–300 ms first-render window. It also does not mention “first render” timing specifically or the 150 ms lower bound.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States multi-line suggestions acceptable at 300-1000ms with boundaries and visual affordances
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that sub-300 ms is ideal for inline suggestions and that multi-line suggestions can tolerate roughly 500–900 ms (and up to 1 s for complex generations), and it recommends boundaries like breakpoint-aligned timing plus visual affordances such as inline ghost text and streaming/progress indicators.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States comment-to-code generation tolerable at 0.5-2 seconds with streaming or progress indicators
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives tolerable latency for CodeWhisperer comment-to-code as “up to 1 second” and elsewhere mentions “500-900 ms” for comment-to-code, and it recommends streaming/progress indicators for high-latency phases. It does not state that comment-to-code generation is tolerable at 0.5–2 seconds specifically.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends streaming, progress indicators, or on-demand mode when generation exceeds timing budgets
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends streaming output and using progress indicators to reduce perceived waiting, and it advises switching to delayed display or on-demand invocation when latency exceeds thresholds (e.g., >500 ms or >1 s). These recommendations appear in the latency-specific fallbacks and latency management sections.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends on-demand or delayed triggers for debugging and proactive acceptable for feature development
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends using on-demand or breakpoint/delayed triggers specifically for debugging (e.g., “on-demand or breakpoint-aligned suggestions favored”) while endorsing more proactive inline suggestions during new feature development (e.g., “Apply proactive AI completions more liberally during new feature development”).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States debugging has shorter edit-run cycles (approximately 1 minute) than feature development (approximately 3 minutes)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses that debugging is more interruption-sensitive than new feature development, but it never states specific edit-run cycle durations. It does not mention debugging having ~1 minute cycles or feature development having ~3 minute cycles, nor any comparable numeric contrast between them.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States less-experienced developers show higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than 10+ year developers
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that acceptance is higher for developers with 2-5 years experience (e.g., Copilot ~32% vs ~26% for 10+ years, and CodeWhisperer higher usage/acceptance among junior/intermediate vs senior), indicating less-experienced developers accept AI suggestions more than 10+ year developers.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares GitHub Copilot inline suggestions, Tabnine multi-line predictions, and CodeWhisperer comment-to-code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly compares GitHub Copilot’s inline suggestions, Tabnine’s multi-line predictions, and Amazon CodeWhisperer’s comment-to-code generation in separate sections, including latency and acceptance-rate discussion for each.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports acceptance rates 25-35% in production deployment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports acceptance rates within the 25–35% range, stating GitHub Copilot acceptance “26-33% overall” and Amazon CodeWhisperer “about 35%.” These are presented as overall/production-like metrics rather than purely hypothetical values.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports Copilot 10-15% acceptance gain OR CodeWhisperer 50-60% faster OR JetBrains 40-50% acceptance increase
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports Amazon CodeWhisperer productivity gains as “57% faster” task completion in section 2.3, which satisfies the criterion option of CodeWhisperer being 50-60% faster.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies trade-off between acceptance-rate optimization and code quality or retention metrics
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses optimizing acceptance rates via proactive timing and breakpoint alignment while also emphasizing trust calibration, verification, and avoiding disruption to preserve code quality (e.g., warnings about interruption costs and the need for explanations/security scans). This frames a trade-off between maximizing acceptance and maintaining quality/retention-related outcomes like trust and reduced cognitive overload.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes conditional decision framework specifying when proactive versus on-demand presentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides a conditional framework distinguishing proactive suggestions (e.g., during new feature development and at natural breakpoints) versus on-demand suggestions (especially during debugging), and further conditions this by developer experience level (2–5 years vs 10+ years).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Extracts vendor-specific deployment techniques with measured outcomes (e.g., filter +50% acceptance)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes vendor-specific techniques (e.g., Copilot configurable delay, Tabnine breakpoint-timed proactive suggestions, CodeWhisperer security scan feedback) and pairs them with measured outcomes such as acceptance rates (26–33%, ~35%, “52% engagement/62% rejection”) and productivity gains (55–73% faster, ~45%).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides experience-level differentiated strategies with specific recommendations per cohort
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly differentiates strategies for 2–5 years vs 10+ years developers, including a cohort table and tailored recommendations on proactive vs on-demand timing, explanation depth, and trust cues. It also ties these cohort-specific strategies to task types (debugging vs new feature development) and latency thresholds.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies escalation paths when timing budgets exceeded (streaming, progress indicators, on-demand)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response specifies what to do when latency budgets are exceeded, including switching to delayed suggestion display or on-demand invocation, and using streaming/partial suggestions with visual progress indicators. It also mentions fallback modes like retries and switching to local/cached models, which constitute escalation paths tied to timing overruns.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes appropriate trust calibration from blind acceptance with UI implementation patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses trust calibration versus overtrust (e.g., avoiding “misplaced trust”), and ties this to concrete UI patterns such as layered/customizable explanations, lightweight trust cues, confidence-based escalation, security overlays, and on-demand vs proactive triggering.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Acknowledges evidence gaps and recommends instrumentation for uncertain areas
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes missing or imprecise evidence (e.g., “No precise acceptance data fully segmented by experience level”) and proposes collecting/monitoring telemetry and metrics (e.g., JetBrains telemetry, monitoring latency/acceptance/flow disruptions) plus fallback mechanisms for high-latency cases, which functions as recommended instrumentation for uncertain areas.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends proactive suggestions as universally beneficial without acknowledging flow state disruption or interruption cost research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes interruption costs and flow disruption (e.g., 10–15 minutes to regain focus, avoiding mid-task interruptions) and recommends breakpoint-aligned or on-demand suggestions rather than claiming proactive suggestions are universally beneficial.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise timing terminology (latency thresholds, render time, flow state) with explicit millisecond or second units
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses explicit timing terminology and includes multiple latency thresholds with units, such as “below 300 milliseconds,” “400-500 ms,” “<100 ms,” “up to 2 seconds,” “500 ms,” and “1 s,” in the context of suggestion rendering and flow disruption.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes findings by tool (Copilot, Tabnine, CodeWhisperer) and cross-cuts by experience levels and task types
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized with separate sections for GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, and Amazon CodeWhisperer, and it repeatedly cross-references differences by experience level (2–5 vs 10+ years) and by task type (debugging vs new feature development), including a summary table combining these dimensions.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective research synthesis tone without marketing language or anecdotal personal experience
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, research-report style with neutral phrasing and focuses on synthesizing studies and metrics. It does not include personal anecdotes or first-person experience, and it largely avoids overt marketing language, sticking to evidence-oriented claims and recommendations.

presentation-quality.requirement: Concludes key sections with specific design recommendations derived from the research evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response ends with a dedicated “Summary and Actionable Design Recommendations” section that provides specific UI/UX recommendations (e.g., sub-300 ms latency targets, breakpoint-aligned timing, proactive vs on-demand by task and experience, explanation layering) explicitly tied to the preceding research claims and metrics.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents comparisons as prose paragraphs rather than using tables, parallel sections, or labeled side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into multiple labeled sections with headings and bullet points, and it includes a side-by-side comparison table for experience levels. It is not primarily presented as prose-only paragraphs for comparisons.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites academic research on programmer interruption costs or AI code completion from arXiv, ACM, IEEE, or ResearchGate
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites academic research from arXiv (e.g., “Developer Interaction Patterns with Proactive AI” and “Experience with GitHub Copilot…”), and also references an ACM venue (Communications of the ACM) for Copilot productivity findings.

citation-quality.requirement: References JetBrains AI assistant deployment metrics or timing research from blog.jetbrains.com or official JetBrains publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response mentions “JetBrains telemetry” but does not cite any JetBrains AI assistant deployment metrics or timing research from blog.jetbrains.com or other official JetBrains publications. All listed sources are non-JetBrains domains, and none reference JetBrains AI assistant deployment timing/metrics.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from github.blog, microsoft.com/research, or ACM publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites an ACM publication (“Measuring GitHub Copilot's Impact on Productivity” in Communications of the ACM) and a Microsoft Research publication hosted on microsoft.com/research. These satisfy the requirement to cite GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from the specified domains.

citation-quality.requirement: References established HCI timing thresholds (Nielsen, speedcurve.com, or equivalent authoritative HCI sources)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites established HCI latency thresholds (e.g., “below 300 milliseconds” and “400–500 ms”) and references an authoritative HCI source via Nielsen’s 0.1/1/10-second-style guidance through Michael Brenndoerfer’s UX/latency discussion, which is an HCI-aligned reference.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes research or metrics from at least 2 of AWS CodeWhisperer, Google Research, Tabnine, or other code completion vendors
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes vendor metrics and discussion for at least two of the specified tools, explicitly covering Tabnine (latency/acceptance/productivity) and Amazon CodeWhisperer (latency/acceptance/productivity), alongside other sources.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations in cited research rather than personal anecdotes or team experiences
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly bases timing and latency guidance on cited sources (e.g., bracketed references for latency thresholds, interruption costs, and breakpoint timing) and does not rely on personal anecdotes or team experiences.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations solely in personal blogs, forums, or social media without citing academic papers or official vendor research
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites a mix of sources including academic papers (e.g., ICSE 2024, arXiv, CACM), official vendor research/blogs (Microsoft Research, AWS blogs, Amazon Science), and other web articles. It is not grounded solely in personal blogs, forums, or social media.
